An insight into the Unrealistic Burden of Being a Brazil Fan Brazil is the only country to have attended every World Cup and is the country to win the most Cups. This is well known. But Brazil is also the country with the 2nd most attendances at the final match (7) and Brazil has not failed to advance from the Group Stage since 1966, plus Brazil is the only country to win two back-to-back World Cups. All this adds up to a crazy amount of expectation of success at the WC, not just from the outside world, but especially from the fans. Nevermind not wining every World Cup, there are many other events that would doom Brazil to be held as a failure: Don't qualify in the top 2 of CONMEBOL? - Failure Don't win every qualifying home game? - Failure Tie a game at the WC? - Failure Not play {insert Barca millionaire name here}? - Failure Not win the Group? - Failure Not score at least one amazing goal per game? - Failure Not put on a Beautiful Game footy clinic in every game? - Failure Win a game 1:nil? - Failure Not take at least 20 shots at goal every game? - Failure This kind pressure has made being a Seleção fan a bummer much of the time. (Porra!) I do think Brazil does best when everyone thinks they will suck.

Pretty cool to see him back with the team and in good spirits. I was watching the TSN nightly wrap up segment last night and they said that three of the top orthopedic surgeons in Vancouver were actually watching the match, saw the injury happen, and almost immediately began coordinating/consulting with one another about preparing for the operation and started heading to the hospital before they even got a call from the people affiliated with the the Canadian soccer program. By the time Koné got to the hospital, they already had their advanced team prep the surgical suite and the surgeons themselves were basically ready to handle business. Given the way they were able to get the injury stabilized, get Koné to the hospital with highly qualified surgeons, and operate so quickly after such a gruesome and potentially career-altering iniury, I'd say the man had some incredible post-injury luck. From the update they gave last night, it sounds like they think his prognosis for his future recovery and return to soccer is good, as well. Even the injury apparently happened in such a way that the fractures and surrounding damage weren't nearly as extensive or as difficult to repair as is often seen in similar cases. All very good news for Koné and Canada.
